\begin{abstract}
\noindent We report a complete verification run of the Econstellar compute
engine, a continuously deployed econometric service for financial-market
analysis in which every public method ships with a pre-registered, failable
evaluation. The suite executed 26 method rows --- spanning
stationarity, unit roots and cointegration; volatility, long memory and spectra; information flow and transfer entropy; connectedness and networks; contagion channels and systemic risk; reproduction and formal verification --- against the live service, each checked against an expected
band fixed before the run and naming its full parameterisation and source:
26 passed, 0 failed, 0 pending.
Every quantity traces to a live computation or a documented published result;
the methods execute the original papers' own software rather than
reimplementations; and the document is generated mechanically from the run
artefact, so that it may be regenerated and audited rather than taken on trust.
Honest negatives, where present, are reported as the registered expectation: an
empty network or a non-significant test is a result, not a blemish to manage.
\end{abstract}

\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{Keywords:} reproducible econometrics; transfer entropy;
financial contagion; systemic risk; sandboxed computation; continuous verification.

\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{JEL classification:} C55, C58, C63, C88, G15.

\section{The engine and the run}
The engine is a sandboxed R/Node compute kernel on Google Cloud Run (service
\code{shssm-compute}, region asia-south1), backed by an always-on workstation
job server for long-running bootstrap and surrogate work. Each method executes
the published package of its source paper rather than a reimplementation. The
run below was produced by \code{evals/run-evals.mjs}, whose output
\code{evals.json} is committed unedited beside the public pages and is the sole
source of every number in this report.

\end{center}

\section{Data and methods}
The engine analyses daily log-returns of major markets. Two panels recur:
\code{g20} (18 markets, 5036 daily observations, 306 directed pairs) and
\code{g20\_24} (24 series, adding commodities, 552 directed pairs).
Information-flow estimators require stationary inputs, so returns --- integrated
of order zero --- are used throughout, never price levels. The 26
method rows group into the families below; each names the published basis it runs.

\begin{description}\setlength{\itemsep}{2pt}
\item[Stationarity, unit roots and cointegration.] Augmented Dickey--Fuller and KPSS tests, the Im--Pesaran--Shin and Levin--Lin--Chu panel unit-root tests, and Johansen's trace test for cointegrating rank. The downstream information-flow estimators require inputs integrated of order zero, so log-returns are tested, never price levels.\\\textit{Methods:} \code{unit\_root}, \code{live\_unit\_root}, \code{panel\_unit\_root}, \code{vecm}.
\item[Volatility, long memory and spectra.] GARCH(1,1) persistence, the Hurst exponent by detrended fluctuation analysis, and a maximal-overlap discrete wavelet transform for scale-resolved variance and cross-market coherence.\\\textit{Methods:} \code{garch}, \code{dfa\_hurst}, \code{namh\_hurst}, \code{wavelet}, \code{wavelet\_coherence}.
\item[Information flow and transfer entropy.] Transfer entropy \cite{schreiber2000} estimated by the Kraskov--St\"ogbauer--Grassberger nearest-neighbour scheme \cite{kraskov2004} in the Frenzel--Pompe conditional-mutual-information form \cite{frenzel2007}, with significance assessed against IAAFT surrogates \cite{schreiber1996} and edges selected under Benjamini--Hochberg false-discovery control \cite{benjamini1995}; a wavelet--quantile variant resolves spillover by scale and quantile.\\\textit{Methods:} \code{ksg\_te}, \code{ksg\_robustness}, \code{namh\_te}, \code{namh\_pipeline}, \code{wqte}, \code{soch\_profile}, \code{quantile\_var}.
\item[Connectedness and networks.] Vector-autoregressive impulse responses, Granger-causal edges, and the Diebold--Y\i lmaz forecast-error-variance connectedness index \cite{dieboldyilmaz2012}, static and rolling, with a dynamic conditional-correlation companion and an \code{igraph} network surface.\\\textit{Methods:} \code{var\_irf}, \code{granger}, \code{connectedness}, \code{spillover\_rolling}, \code{network}, \code{rolling\_dcc}.
\item[Contagion channels and systemic risk.] Attribution of crisis-window spillovers to economic channels (trade, finance and others; the global-financial-crisis decomposition is anchored to arXiv:2604.26546), and a daily systemic-risk index recomputed in full from the stored panel.\\\textit{Methods:} \code{channel\_attribution}, \code{sri\_daily}.
\item[Reproduction and formal verification.] A delta-verifier that re-runs the published estimators of the \code{namh} package \cite{namh2026} and reports the maximum absolute deviation from the archived output, and a seeded robustness badge \cite{lecuyer1999} over the published SOCH symmetry test.\\\textit{Methods:} \code{namh\_reproduce}, \code{soch\_robustness}.
\end{description}

\section{Results}
Table~\ref{tab:results} groups the 26 rows by family, placing each
verified value beside the pre-registered band it was checked against:
26 of 26 fall within band (0 fail,
0 pending). The bands are not decorative. Several encode
honest negatives: where the engine ships an empty false-discovery-controlled
network or a bit-exact reproduction target, the null or the exact value is the
correct outcome, and a populated network or a moved number would be the failure,
not the pass.

\section{Failures and pending rows}
All 26 rows fall within their pre-registered bands. No band was
widened and no extraction was altered after the run; the tables here are the
unedited rendering of a single execution of the committed runner.

\section{The deployment chain}
This run closed a release cycle that moved the engine's public registry from
23 to 26 methods. Three registrations landed together: \code{namh\_pipeline}
(a seeded end-to-end surrogate pipeline run as an asynchronous workstation
job), \code{namh\_reproduce} (a $\Delta$-verifier that re-runs the published
NAMH estimators and reports the maximum absolute deviation from the paper's
archived output), and \code{soch\_robustness} (a badge-mode stability grid
over the published SOCH-B symmetry test, the subject of
Section~\ref{sec:case}).

The chain was: engine push (commits \code{37d4337..5d0dc80}, with the
post-fix commit \code{f1616f2} following), source deploy to Cloud Run
revision \code{shssm-compute-00033-wq2}, an environment-variable restore to
revision \code{00034-zqz}, and --- after the driver fix described below --- a
second deploy arriving at revision \code{shssm-compute-00036-llf}, which this
report's suite ran against. Two operational details are worth the record.
First, the deploy script's API-key lookup missed because the repository had
moved; the documented remedy (an in-place service update, no rebuild)
restored chat capability in under a minute, and the miss was loudly warned by
the script itself --- a designed failure surface behaving as designed.
Second, the suite's single-shot 15-second health probe once recorded a live
engine as unreachable while all 26 method rows passed against it: the probe
had raced a Cloud Run cold start. The probe now retries once with a longer
window; the dead-engine path (a deliberately unreachable URL must produce a
non-zero exit and honest red rows) still passes.

\section{Case study: a stochastic gate caught by its own evaluation}
\label{sec:case}
The most instructive event of the run was a failure. The
\code{soch\_robustness} row re-runs the published SOCH-B direction-symmetry
test (the \code{sochcontagion} package's own \code{soch\_test\_symmetry},
GPL-3 --- the paper's code, not a reimplementation) over a nuisance grid
$\tau \in \{0.05, 0.10\} \times J \in \{4, 5\}$ on the eight advanced markets
(28 unordered pairs per configuration, 112 grid points), with a
200-replication bootstrap null. Its expected band was pre-registered from the
Phase-31 closure board: the baseline cell ($\tau=0.05$, $J=4$) holds
$28/28$ pairs --- the paper's ground truth --- with an overall pass rate of
at least $0.90$.

The first genuine end-to-end execution of this driver returned $27/28$ at
baseline and an overall pass rate of $0.9821$ ($110/112$), and the row went
red. Investigation showed the driver carried no random-number-generator pin:
the bootstrap null's 95th-percentile threshold was stochastic across runs,
and one near-boundary pair sat close enough to its threshold to flip between
executions. The Phase-31 grid run had measured $0.9911$ ($111/112$) --- one
grid point away --- consistent with the same knife edge landing on the other
side.

The repair followed the engine's existing seeded-determinism precedent
(\code{namh\_pipeline}): the driver now pins \code{RNGkind("L'Ecuyer-CMRG")}
with seed 42 \cite{lecuyer1999}, and reports both in its output. The band
was not touched. A fresh anchor run (job \code{job\_20260612\_fe0c4f06},
773.5\,s) returned baseline $28/28$, pass rate $0.9911$, badge
\emph{robust} --- the paper's ground truth and the Phase-31 measurement
reproduced exactly under the pin. The full suite was then re-run from
scratch, and its independently submitted job
(\code{job\_20260612\_c0398d0f}) returned the identical tuple, digit for
digit: two independent executions now verify determinism end to end. The
evaluation row was tightened, not widened --- it now requires the exact
seeded pass rate ($0.9911$), the exact baseline count ($28/28$), and the
seed itself ($42$) to be echoed in the result.

Three points generalise beyond this engine. First, binary bootstrap-gated
claims of the form ``the pair holds symmetry'' sit on resampling knife edges
at modest replication counts; at $B=200$, a single marginal pair is enough to
move a headline count. A reproducibility statement for such a claim is
meaningful only with the full tuple --- seed, generator, replication count,
filter, grid, and market set --- stated. Second, grid resolution bounds the
interpretability of badge rates: with 112 points, one flip moves the rate by
$1/112 \approx 0.0089$, so $0.9821$ versus $0.9911$ is a one-point
difference, not a qualitative change; counts should accompany rates. Third,
the institutional lesson: the gate that failed was not noise to be routed
around but the system working --- a pre-registered band over a first-run
driver surfaced a latent nondeterminism that would otherwise have produced
intermittent, unexplainable reds in nightly operation.

\section{Formal and epistemic layers}
Two verification layers beyond the numerical suite are active. The formal
layer (Lean~4 with mathlib, toolchain v4.30.0) machine-checks the SOCH
spectral results: 11 of 12 declarations are Lean-accepted, including the
unconditional closure of the product-Lorentzian spectral density
proposition ($\Vert H(\omega)\Vert^{2} = S(\omega)$) and an
existence-and-uniqueness chain for the first-order-condition frequency; one
declaration (the analytic half of the peak-location lemma) honestly carries
\code{sorry} and is reported as unproved. The epistemic layer --- a
provenance-carrying claims ledger with twenty seeded claims spanning
established, contested, and superseded states --- is built and its public
read surface is deployed; its data store provisioning remains a gated
operator action, and until it runs the API degrades honestly, naming the
missing dataset rather than fabricating an empty success.

\section{Implications}
For applied work, the run's substantive anchors held without exception:
the India ADF statistic ($-49.18$), the GARCH persistence ($\alpha + \beta =
0.991$), the named-panel IPS statistic ($-77.26$ on
\{India, USA, UK, China, Japan\}), the NAMH transfer-entropy window mean
($-0.22322165$, inside its pre-registered band), the KSG USA$\to$Japan
transfer entropy ($0.154$), the KSG robustness Spearman ($0.7282$), the
GFC trade-channel share ($27.9\%$, arXiv:2604.26546), and the live systemic
risk index ($0.00849$). The honest negatives also held: the FDR-gated NAMH
network retains $0/552$ edges and is reported as a degenerate network
(amber), and the SOCH-C significance test remains not significant
($p=0.105$) --- both immutable facts of the record, not blemishes to be
managed. The engine's claim is not that its numbers are impressive but that
they are traceable: every figure in this report resolves to a job
identifier, a revision string, or a published artefact, and the pipeline
that produced this document will produce the next one unattended.

\section{Reproducibility and integrity}\label{sec:repro}
This report is generated mechanically from the run artefact and inherits its
discipline. (i)~Every row carries a pre-registered band naming its full
parameterisation and a source, fixed before the run. (ii)~\code{evals.json} is
the output of one genuine run of the committed runner and is never hand-edited;
to change a number one re-runs the suite, not the report. (iii)~A band failure
halts work --- bands are never widened to pass, and only extraction plumbing
(the harness misreading a result shape) may be calibrated, with the band
untouched. (iv)~Honest negatives are permanent: a non-significant test is
reported as non-significant, an empty false-discovery network as empty
\cite{benjamini1995}, an unproved formal statement as unproved.
(v)~Asynchronous rows are real workstation jobs with persistent identifiers, or
are reported pending --- never fabricated.

Reproduction is graded rather than asserted. Deterministic estimators --- the
nearest-neighbour transfer entropy \cite{kraskov2004} and the Hurst panel of
the published \code{namh} package \cite{namh2026} --- are reproduced to a
stated maximum absolute deviation and earn a green only on an exact match;
stochastic surrogate stages, whose generators were unseeded in the original
runs, are reported amber rather than dressed as exact. Where the engine seeds
its own surrogate and bootstrap stages it uses a single declared generator
\cite{lecuyer1999}, so a result is reproducible for a fixed seed and core count
and the asymmetry with the unseeded published run is stated, not concealed. The
engine and this verification apparatus are described in full in the accompanying
system paper \cite{econstellar2026}.
